hypothesis: On the 300k-line app.log fixture, in analyzer.py's analyze(), replacing the O(k^2) seen_requests list-membership scan (k = ERROR-line count) with a set is the single change that recovers the most wall-clock time among four candidate suspects (regex recompiled every line, seen_requests list scan, dict-item string concatenation for the error report, and a per-line list re-sort for top-10 latencies), i.e. an ablation that fixes each suspect alone one at a time identifies fix_seen_requests as recovering the largest fraction of baseline runtime.

On the 300k-line app.log fixture, in analyzer.py's analyze(), replacing the O(k^2) seen_requests list-membership scan (k = ERROR-line count) with a set is the single change that recovers the most wall-clock time among four candidate suspects (regex recompiled every line, seen_requests list scan, dict-item string concatenation for the error report, and a per-line list re-sort for top-10 latencies), i.e. an ablation that fixes each suspect alone one at a time identifies fix_seen_requests as recovering the largest fraction of baseline runtime.

configurations: CPython 3.13.13; analyzer.py analyze() replica; fixture-a-research/app.log, 300000 lines, seed=7 (72% INFO/13% WARN/15% ERROR, unique request id per line); ablation: one suspect fixed at a time, all others left as in the original; wall-clock median of 3 repeats per variant, file cache warmed before timing; every variant's output (error counts, top-10 latencies, report text) checked byte-identical to the baseline replica's output; fixing seen_requests alone recovered ~76-79% of baseline runtime vs ~5-23% for the other three suspects individually
effect: dominant cause of analyzer.py slowness on the 300k-line app.log fixture: seen_requests list-membership scan (O(k^2) in ERROR-line count) vs. three other candidate suspects (regex recompiled per line, dict-item string concatenation for the error report, per-line list re-sort of top-10 latencies)
